policy_title: Closing the Loop: A Three-Tier Framework to Divert 1M Tons of Food Waste
tagline: First causal evidence that bans without infrastructure fail — the Massachusetts model shows how to fix it

problem_stats.label: Sent to Landfill
problem_stats.context: 38.5% of all food waste — largest single destination

problem_stats.label: Residential Donations
problem_stats.context: Despite $141B in surplus food and 23.5M tons

problem_stats.label: GHG Emissions
problem_stats.context: Equivalent to 48 million cars on the road

problem_stats.label: Meals Wasted
problem_stats.context: While 44 million Americans face food insecurity

diversion_model.insight: State-level infrastructure is 6x more important than ban status for predicting diversion success

did_result.interpretation: Organic waste bans are associated with a 2.03 pp increase in landfill rate — bans alone do not reduce landfilling
did_result.method: Two-way fixed effects DiD with state-clustered SEs
did_result.key_insight: Independently validated by UCSD study in Science (2024): only Massachusetts succeeded, because it paired mandates with processing infrastructure

diversion_rate_did.interpretation: Bans associated with 5.18 pp decrease in diversion rate — mandates without infrastructure worsen outcomes

event_study.parallel_trends: Supported — pre-treatment coefficients (t=-5 to t=-2) are statistically indistinguishable from zero
event_study.pattern: Effects grow over time post-adoption, reaching 4.72 pp at t=6

synthetic_control_california.interpretation: California's landfill rate increased 2.42 pp relative to synthetic counterfactual after SB 1383

covid_resilience.interpretation: Ban states showed greater resilience during COVID — institutional infrastructure provides real shock absorption

tier1_federal.name: Food Date Labeling Act (S.2541/H.R.4987)
tier1_federal.mechanism: Standardize to 'BEST if Used By' (quality) and 'USE By' (safety)
tier1_federal.evidence: ReFED cost-benefit analysis; CA AB 660 as state model (70K tons, $300M savings)
tier1_federal.waste_targeted: 3.83M tons from Date Label Concerns (Retail: 2.18M, Foodservice: 0.40M)

tier1_federal.name: National Organic Waste Mandate + Infrastructure Fund
tier1_federal.mechanism: Binding federal mandate paired with processing facility investment
tier1_federal.evidence: DiD coefficient magnitude (2.03 pp) applied to 38 non-ban states; Massachusetts model validation
tier1_federal.key_lesson: UCSD Science study: mandates work ONLY with infrastructure — MA succeeded, 4 other states failed

tier2_state.name: Expand Infrastructure-Backed Bans to 38 States
tier2_state.priority_targets: FL (85.7K tons), TX (82.6K tons), WI (54.2K tons), GA (37.5K tons), AZ (37.5K tons)
tier2_state.cluster_insight: 22 'Landfill-Dependent' states have 53.9% avg landfill rate — highest-impact targets

tier2_state.name: Municipal Donation & Composting Infrastructure
tier2_state.mechanism: Curbside organics collection + community food recovery hubs
tier2_state.evidence: Composting handles only 19.3% of waste (11.5M tons); France mandate: 20%+ donation increase, 46K tons/yr rescued

tier3_sector.action: Curbside composting + standardized labels + consumer education
tier3_sector.data_point: 33% of surplus, $141B value, 11.2M tons to landfill, 0 donations
tier3_sector.top_causes: Inedible Parts (6.8M), Spoiled (5.9M), Didn't Want Leftovers (2.3M)

tier3_sector.action: Waste tracking mandates for businesses above threshold
tier3_sector.data_point: 42% of landfill waste (9.7M tons) despite only 18% of surplus

tier3_sector.action: Gleaning incentives + secondary markets + buyer rejection reduction
tier3_sector.data_point: 14.8M tons waste, 13.4M tons not harvested, lowest landfill rate (0.83%)
tier3_sector.top_causes: Left Behind After Harvest (13.4M across 3 subcategories), Buyer Rejections (0.6M)

Agricultural States.profile: Lowest landfill rate, high farm share, policy leaders

Landfill-Dependent.profile: Highest landfill rate — top priority for ban + infrastructure expansion

Diversion Leaders.profile: High diversion through agricultural infrastructure without mandates

Mixed Economy States.profile: Moderate landfill rate — second priority tier for expansion

evidence_citations.finding: Bans increase landfill rate by 2.03 pp (p=0.008) — mandates alone are insufficient
Parallel pre-trends confirmed; effects grow to 4.72 pp by year 6 post-adoption
California's landfill rate 2.42 pp above counterfactual after SB 1383
State infrastructure is #1 predictor of diversion (SHAP=0.177); ban_active is #7 for landfill (0.025)
22 'Landfill-Dependent' states (53.9% avg rate) are highest-priority targets
Combined framework diverts 1.02M tons, saves $6.37B, prevents 4.47M MT CO2e
Only MA succeeded among 5 ban states — infrastructure is the key differentiator
Date labels: 425K tons, $1.82B net benefit — most cost-effective single intervention
350K tons diverted, $194M economic value, 1,670+ jobs from infrastructure-backed ban
51-57% food waste recovery rate; 13% tonnage reduction; 40% donation increase
First state date label law; projected 70K tons, $300M savings in CA alone
20%+ donation increase, 10M additional meals/yr from supermarket mandate
DiD is the gold standard for policy evaluation; our analysis addresses identified gap in food waste causal evidence

competition_differentiators: First DiD evaluation of food waste bans using ReFED state panel dataset
Finding independently validated by peer-reviewed Science publication (UCSD 2024)
Intellectually honest: we report that bans alone fail, then show WHY and HOW to fix it
Three-tier framework grounded in causal evidence, not correlations
Every number in the brief traces to a specific data source or model output
SHAP interpretability confirms policy mechanism: infrastructure > mandates
DiD-calibrated scenarios with state-by-state projections for all 38 non-ban states
